data.title: GSK: 'Strong Buy' On Mo-Rez Oncology Expansion Potential
data.description:
The last time I wrote about GSK (GSK), it was with a Seeking Alpha article entitled "GlaxoSmithKline's Priority Review For Cancer Drug Sets It Up For First Of A Kind FDA Approval." With respect to this article, I mentioned that the company was in the process of gearing up to potentially receive FDA approval of belantamab [now marketed as BLENREP]. It was approved in combination with bortezomib and dexamethasone for the treatment of adults with relapsed/refractory multiple myeloma [MM] who have received at least two prior lines of therapy, including that of a proteasome inhibitor and an immunomodulatory agent. I had a "Strong Buy" set then, and I am going to keep this rating for the time being. There are several reasons why I don't believe a downgrade is in order at this present time.

The first reason is because the company is already in the process of looking to expand its market potential with BLENREP. It did have a problem with having the drug pulled off the market for several years but has been able to make a comeback in combination with bortezomib and dexamethasone in a narrower MM market. However, the company does boast that it can compete well because it was able to beat Johnson & Johnson's (JNJ) DARZALEX in a head-to-head study in terms of overall survival [OS]. Plus, it is the only anti-BCMA agent that can be administered across multiple healthcare settings, including community centers [up to 70% of patients are addressable in a community setting]. It is currently evaluating the use of it in the ongoing DREAMM-7 and DREAMM-10 studies to potentially expand the label of the drug to go after both 2nd-line and 1st-line MM patients, respectively. However, the downside is that the current process is that the DREAMM-10 trial is only currently recruiting patients. Plus, the DREAMM-7 study using BLENREP to treat patients with 2nd-line MM is expected to have an OS data release in 2028. It has a long way to go, with sales only coming in at $22.9 million in 2025. However, the expectation is that it could eventually possibly obtain peak sales of around $4 billion. The expansion opportunities of targeting 2nd-line and 1st-line MM patients could potentially help to achieve this goal.

The second reason is the sales growth of its Oncology portfolio, which in 2025 saw massive growth. Revenues for this segment reached $2.7 billion, which was a growth rate of 43%. Besides BLENREP, the company in this portfolio has JEMPERLI and OJJAARA. Both of these candidates achieved percentage revenue growth of 89% and 60%, respectively. Its Respiratory, Immunology & Inflammation portfolio reached sales of $5.13 billion, which was a yearly growth rate of 18%. This is to potentially be helped in terms of an acquisition it made, which was the ability to buy RAPT Therapeutics for $2.2 billion to bring in ozureprubart as an anti-IgE antibody for the treatment of food allergies. I wrote about this buyout in a prior Seeking Alpha article entitled "RAPT Therapeutics: A Buy As GSK Acquisition Highlights Long-Acting IgE Candidate."

This brings me to the third reason why I believe that this stock should be kept at a "Strong Buy" rating, which is a huge push to be bolder with respect to phase 3 clinical testing. This drug candidate, known as Mo-Rez [mocertatug rezetecan], is a B7-H4 antibody drug conjugate [ADC] being developed to target patients with endometrial cancer [EC] and ovarian cancer [OC]. More about this below, but it plans several late-stage studies for this program, and if successful, it believes that it could reach blockbuster potential. With massive growth observed from its Oncology portfolio in 2025, plus the ability to make an aggressive push in several late-stage studies with Mo-Rez for EC/OC, I believe that it should retain its "Strong Buy" rating.

BEHOLD-1 Data Bodes Well For Advancement Of Mo-Rez Into Two Large Market Indications

As I mentioned above, GlaxoSmithKline is doing very well in terms of growth for its Oncology portfolio, which grew the most in the Specialty Medicines Category for it. What I'm about to go over now is the ability for the company to potentially develop another blockbuster market opportunity. This would be with respect to evaluating the use of B7-H4 candidate Mo-Rez for the treatment of patients with endometrial cancer and platinum-resistant ovarian cancer. As a matter of fact, it is going to run an extensive program involving 5 global pivotal phase 3 trials in 2026. Not only that, but it was able to report positive results from its phase 1 BEHOLD-1 study using this candidate to treat both of these patient populations. Before going over the clinical data from this early-stage trial, plus any catalysts to be released from this program, I believe that it is first important to go over what these cancers are and what the possible market opportunity for the company could end up being.

The thing is that the targeting of both of these cancers makes sense because they have high recurrence rates with advanced disease. Such recurrence is observed in up to 67% of endometrial cancer patients and 70% of ovarian cancer patients. Endometrial cancer [EC] is characterized as the growth of abnormal cells and the formation of a solid tumor in the uterus of a patient. Specifically, this cancer is in terms of the cells that line the uterus and in the upper part of it. That's because cancer that forms near the cervix [bottom part of the uterus] is known as cervical cancer. Some symptoms that these patients might experience are:

  • Pain in the pelvic area
  • Abnormal vaginal bleeding
  • Change in menstrual cycle

The global endometrial cancer market size is projected to reach $40.66 billion by 2031. This is one large market opportunity that the company could potentially go after. The other one involves developing B7-H4 ADC Mo-Rez for the treatment of patients with ovarian cancer. First and foremost, ovarian cancer [OC], as the name suggests, is the formation of a tumor on the ovaries of a patient. The two ovaries in place are responsible for producing eggs and, in addition, for the hormones estrogen and progesterone. This cancer is devastating because it can spread to nearby tissues as well. Plus, in the early stages of disease, there are no symptoms that occur. What's even worse is that even when symptoms are present, they might mimic those of another disease. The ovarian cancer market, in the 7 major markets, is expected to reach $5.7 billion in 2034. This is a pretty good market, but it is important to note something immediately, which is that a specific group of patients is being targeted. The goal is to only go after patients with platinum-resistant disease, or platinum-resistant ovarian cancer [PROC]. PROC is defined as a patient with ovarian cancer who has a recurrence of the disease within a 6 month period after having received multiple lines of chemotherapy.

The thing with Mo-Rez is that it is an anti-B7-H4 candidate that specifically targets the immune checkpoint protein B7-H4 expressed on a variety of solid tumors. What makes this a promising target is that it is widely expressed in tumors but has limited expression in healthy tissues, making it a prime target. The main thing about B7-H4 is that it suppresses the immune system. How so? Well, there are three key reasons why:

  • T-cell exhaustion
  • Substantial increase in Regulatory T cells [Tregs]
  • Recruitment of tumor-associated macrophages [TAMs]

Another reason why the targeting of ovarian cancer and endometrial cancer makes sense is because B7-H4 is highly expressed in them. Consider that B7-H4 expression in OC and EC is said to be 94.5% and 71.5%, respectively. Mo-Rez is an anti-B7-H4 monoclonal antibody covalently linked to a topoisomerase inhibitor payload.

This push by GlaxoSmithKline is bold, but it is necessary for it to start to build further growth for its portfolio. To accomplish such a move, it is expected to initiate 5 pivotal global phase 3 trials in 2026. This is to be split into three ovarian cancer trials and then two endometrial cancer trials. The company has good standing to start these late-stage studies; it didn't just decide to start them out of nowhere. The reason for deciding to initiate this extensive late-stage program with 5 pivotal global phase 3 studies is because of the positive findings it observed in the two-part, open-label phase 1 BEHOLD-1 study evaluating the use of this B7-H4 ADC in the targeting of these patients with PROC and advanced/recurrent EC. It is important to state that this trial was split into two different parts, as I just noted:

  • Phase 1a - assessed 4 dose levels of intravenous Mo-Rez given once every 3 weeks [Q3W] until disease progression or toxicity ensued
  • Phase 1b - dose expansion cohort of study where PROC or EC patients were randomized to either two-thirds or two Mo-Rez doses, respectively

The bottom line is that this big pharma was able to achieve highly impressive confirmed objective response rates [cORR] for this clinical trial. In PROC patients, it was noted that patients who took the highest dose of Mo-Rez obtained a cORR of 62%. Then, when a high dose of this drug was given to recurrent or advanced EC patients, the cORR was 67%. Besides this, there were other highly important findings that were mentioned. The first of which is that these response rates obtained were those of broad B7-H4 expression potential. This provides the proof that the company could go after a broad set of these patients with gynecological cancers [provides proof of concept in targeting them].

Secondly, there were response rates achieved in this trial that were said to be higher than other ADCs in clinical development. Along with the fact that such cORR was achieved, was done under the guise of barely any patients needing to discontinue therapy. Mo-Rez, as part of the phase 3 BEHOLD program, is only the beginning for GlaxoSmithKline [GlaxoSmithKline Q4 and Full-Year 2025 Corporate Presentation, February 4, 2026, slide #18]. The reason why is because it has an extensive amount of trials for its Oncology pipeline of targeting patients with hematological cancers and solid tumors. In addition, the company is not only stopping with the development of B7-H4 ADC Mo-Rez for the treatment of patients with gynecological cancers. It is also in the process of evaluating the use of the B7-H3 ADC risvutatug rezetecan [GSK'227] for the treatment of patients with small-cell lung cancer [SCLC] in the phase 3 EMBOLD study. The development of Mo-Rez is one reason for my "Strong Buy" rating, as it has massive potential to help treat the two unmet medical needs of PROC and EC with Mo-Rez. If that wasn't enough, consider that should this company be successful in some or most of its phase 3 clinical studies, it believes that peak annual market sales potential for this drug in these two indications could end up being $2.7 billion.

Financials

GlaxoSmithKline is continuing to grow its overall sales. It was noted that in 2025 it was able to generate total sales of $44.22 billion, which was a 7% increase from the year prior. The company reports on three areas that it highlights each quarter it reports revenues. These categories are:

  • Specialty Medicines
  • Vaccines
  • General Medicine

Specialty Medicines is the category that saw the largest growth of 17% for the full-year 2025. I brought this up because there was the subcategory in Specialty Medicines where it saw the largest growth, which was that of its Oncology portfolio, which grew by 43%. BLENREP is approved in 15 markets and starting off okay. However, the largest growth drivers here for this Oncology portfolio would be JEMPERLI, which grew by 89%, and then OJJAARA, by 60%. The notion here is that this portfolio is helping this company at the present time and that the addition of Mo-Rez plus the other oncology candidates being advanced above for hematology/solid tumors could further enhance this revenue percentage growth. Of course, all are in clinical trials, and there is still the risk factor, but the potential for it to expand this segment is possible. It has reaffirmed its sales outlook for 2026 with continued growth, with core EPS expected to grow between 7% and 9%. This big pharma is expected to release its Q1 2026 financial report on April 29, 2026. There is an expectation that its EPS will range between $1.19 and $1.20 and revenue will be around $7.75 billion.

Risks To Business

There are several risks that investors should be aware of before investing in GlaxoSmithKline. The first risk to consider would be in terms of the phase 3 BEHOLD program, which is expected to have 5 pivotal phase 3 studies initiated in 2026. This program is expected to evaluate the use of B7-H4 ADC Mo-Rez for the treatment of patients with PROC and advanced/recurrent EC. It was able to achieve a cORR of 62% in PROC patients and then 67% in advanced/recurrent EC patients. The first risk here, first and foremost, is that there is no assurance that the cORRs achieved in the phase 1 BEHOLD-1 study for these indications are going to be replicated in the several expected phase 3 studies for this program. The company has shifted to an aggressive nature in clinical development going forward, and this starts with the extensive phase 3 BEHOLD program. If the company is not able to effectively develop Mo-Rez for the treatment of patients with gynecological cancers, then it will not likely be able to meet its expectation of generating peak annual sales of $2.7 billion for this drug.

The second risk to consider would be in terms of its Specialty Medicines Portfolio achieving a boost thanks to its Oncology Portfolio of products. The Oncology unit grew by 43% in full-year 2025. This was in large part thanks to the percentage growth of JEMPERLI by 89% and OJJAARA by 60%. The risk here is that there is no assurance that one or both of these products are going to allow the company to achieve the same growth in the Oncology unit in the next quarter to be reported, especially in 2026. In turn, this could end up potentially lowering the percentage growth of the Specialty Medicines Portfolio. The flip side is that the other two Portfolios are in place to potentially pick up the slack. The Respiratory, Immunology & Inflammation [RI&I] Portfolio achieved 18% growth to $5.13 billion in full-year 2025. The acquisition of RAPT Therapeutics to obtain ozureprubart for the treatment of food allergies will only eventually serve to help this segment.

The third and final risk to consider would be in terms of its HIV product portfolio. This is important for the company, because in full-year 2025, it was able to increase sales of this portfolio by 11% to $10.39 billion. This was thanks to Dovato and the ability to start developing long-acting injectables. The problem is that the active ingredient in many of its HIV drugs, dolutegravir, is expected to lose patent exclusivity in 2027 and 2028. The risk here is that the company could lose out on its patents and see a steep drop in sales. The hope is to counter this by using long-acting HIV injectables and the Oncology Portfolio advancement I went over above. However, the risk here is that there is no assurance that the sales driven in the Oncology Portfolio will be enough to counter the loss of the patents for dolutegravir.

Conclusion

GlaxoSmithKline is an ideal big pharma company because despite the upcoming loss of patent exclusivity for dolutegravir as part of its HIV portfolio, it is attempting to make a pivot over to many other programs. I noted already that it was able to achieve an acquisition of RAPT Therapeutics to bring in ozureprubart for the treatment of patients with food allergies. What I also went over in this article was seeing massive growth in its Oncology unit Portfolio thanks to revenue percentage growth of JEMPERLI and OJJAARA. The hope is that the long-acting HIV portfolio and the Oncology unit, plus several other measures deployed by the company, will be enough to counter the loss of patent exclusivity of its HIV franchise.

The solid growth of its Oncology Portfolio is one reason why I believe that I should maintain a "strong buy" rating on this stock. It isn't this factor only; it is also because it has several other good things going for it. For instance, just this past week, it was able to deliver promising results from its phase 1 BEHOLD-1 study using Mo-Rez to treat patients with PROC and recurrent/advanced EC. The mode of action of this anti-B7-H4 ADC was proved with the 62% and 67% cORRs observed in these patients, respectively.

What I also didn't mention is that the company, along with its partner Ionis Pharmaceuticals (IONS), was able to achieve positive results from two studies known as B-Well 1 and B-Well 2 using antisense oligonucleotide [ASO] bepirovirsen for the treatment of patients with Chronic Hepatitis B [CHB]. A regulatory review for this drug in China for the treatment of these patients was accepted for review, with other regulatory submissions likely to follow in 2026. With extensive percentage growth of 43% for full-year 2025 achieved for its Oncology Portfolio thanks to JEMPERLI and OJJAARA, plus the bold move to move over to the development of B7-H4 ADC Mo-Rez for the treatment of patients with PROC/EC, I believe that it is important to maintain a "Strong Buy" rating for now.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: MPLX: Upgrading To Bullish As The Gulf Coast Build-Out Meets The AI Power
data.description:
MPLX LP (MPLX) has been an incredible investment for many income investors over the years, as it has consistently produced a high single-digit yield while moving significantly higher from its pre-pandemic levels. I’m bullish on MPLX going forward. It's coming off a record 2025, where it generated $7 billion in adjusted EBITDA and returned $4.4 billion to shareholders through its capital allocation program. There is a lot to look forward to as MPLX’s investment cycle in 2026, where they are deploying roughly $5.5 billion, should create several catalysts to produce larger levels of profitability as the Harmon Creek III, Secretariat II, Titan Sour Gas Phase 2, and the BANGL expansion projects are placed into service. The Ras Laffan export capacity that was taken offline in Qatar due to the geopolitical conflict should reinforce the structural case for U.S. Gulf Coast LNG and LPG exports, for which Permian gas becomes the feedstock and MPLX owns many assets. MPLX is a strong partnership with fee-based cash flows, long-term take-or-pay contracts, and a strong capital plan allocation plan that shareholders should benefit from. I am bullish on MPLX going forward, as the distribution yield is vastly superior to the risk-free rate of return, and the recent energy crisis could make the market realize how important energy infrastructure is to the economy.

Following up on my previous article about MPLX

Back in December I had written an article on MPLX (can be read here) where I discussed why I was downgrading my investment thesis to neutral. I felt that MPLX traded at the highest multiples in its peer group, with its price-to-EBITDA at 9.55x and price-to-operating cash flow at 9.33x. The yield was strong, but there were other opportunities that made more sense from a value proposition standpoint. Even though the future growth from Permian and NGL expansions was interesting, I felt the upside was priced in. Since then, units of MPLX have remained basically flat as they have increased by 0.47%, while the S&P 500 has gained 4.17%. I am upgrading my thesis on MPLX because I believe that the geopolitical conflict with Iran reminded everyone how important energy infrastructure is and that a lot of things have changed since my last article that warrant an investment upgrade on MPLX from me.

A lot of things have changed since Q3, and the geopolitical landscape has benefited MPLX

MPLX provided investors with its second consecutive 12.5% distribution increase, which brought the annualized payout to $4.31. Management has committed to similar increases in the future, as they are promising that they can deliver on the coverage ratio. MPLX executed $5.5 billion of M&A activity as the Northwind Midstream acquisition ($2.4 billion, Delaware Basin sour gas treating), the BANGL buyout ($703 million for the remaining 55%), the Whiptail Midstream pick-up ($237 million), and the additional Matterhorn Express interest ($151 million) allowed MPLX to expand their wellhead-to-water playbook. MPLX divested $1 billion of assets in the Rockies to Harvest Midstream in November, which is an indication that the management team isn't going to focus on assets that aren’t strategic to their future profitability. MPLX generated $7 billion of adjusted EBITDA, $4.9 billion of net income, and distributable cash flow (DCF) of $5.8 billion in 2025, which is strong enough to maintain their current capital allocation program while reinvesting in their assets.

While management has set MPLX up for success, the geopolitical conflict, as unfortunate as it is, puts MPLX in a stronger position. On March 18th, Qatar's Ras Laffan Industrial City was impacted by drone and missile strikes. QatarEnergy subsequently disclosed damage to two liquefaction trains, which was roughly 17% of Qatari LNG capacity. The long-term impact is estimated to be up to 5 years of repairs. Qatar is the second largest country in the global LNG export business, behind the U.S. The commodities market response was immediate, and U.S. LNG exports hit a record 11.7 million metric tons in March 2026, which was roughly 1.8 million tons above the prior-year mark. The EIA's February outlook has U.S. LNG exports exceeding 18.1 Bcf/d by 2027, up from 15 Bcf/d in 2025, with new capacity coming on from Corpus Christi Stage 3, Golden Pass, and eventually Plaquemines expansions.

The reality is that as heartbreaking as the situation is, it was bullish for MPLX, as Permian gas production is forecast to grow from around 22 Bcf/d to 28 Bcf/d in 2026. This represents roughly a fifth of total U.S. natural gas output. Once gas is produced, it needs somewhere to go; it has to be treated, processed, fractionated and either burned in a power plant or liquefied for export. MPLX operates a tremendous amount of infrastructure that should benefit from the expected growth in production domestically. There is a chance that it will take more than 5 years for the Qatari infrastructure to come back online, and some of the damage could become permanent. The U.S. Gulf Coast export infrastructure and the midstream names such as MPLX should become more valuable as they are the backbone of the entire industry. I don’t believe that this is priced into a unit of MPLX yet and is creating an opportunity.

2026 should be a turning point for MPLX, and I am becoming more bullish

MPLX beat the consensus estimates on the top and bottom line as their revenue in Q4 came in at $3.25 billion, which was 20 million ahead of expectations, with EPS of $1.17. The Crude Oil and Products Logistics segment posted a $52 million Q4 EBITDA gain while the underlying pipeline volumes increased 1%, which is bullish for fee-based businesses such as MPLX. This allowed the DCF to come in at $5.8 billion, which grew 2% YoY and created a distribution coverage ratio of 1.3x. Even though the natural gas and NGL services were offset by the divestitures in the Rockies, MPLX’s gathering volumes increased 2%. I believe that 2025 will be looked at as a turning point for MPLX because their interest expenses increased 21% in Q4 to $277 million due to their M&A cycle. The reality is that I would rather see MPLX at 3.7x leverage funding $5.5 billion of strategic deals than at 3.0x leverage having passed on Northwind Midstream and BANGL. Management is focused on the future, and I believe that what happened overseas will validate their decision to lean in toward energy infrastructure assets that have extended lifecycles and decades of profitability ahead of them, as 2026 should be a commissioning year.

The 2026 project list is where MPLX’s strategic investments start to pay off. In the second half of 2026, there are 6 projects coming online, including the Secretariat II processing plant, Harmon Creek III, Titan Complex Phase 2, BANGL pipeline expansion, Bay Runner Pipeline, Blackcomb Pipeline. MPLX has guided that its capex will total $2.7 billion in 2026, which will consist of $2.4 billion of growth assets and $300 million in maintenance. What is interesting is that MPLX used to be known as an MLP that was focused on crude, but roughly 90% of their growth spending is being allocated towards natural gas and NGL services. I believe that the capital that gets deployed this year will have a dramatic impact on 2027’s cash flow profile. Personally, I think 2028–2029 is where the math gets interesting because the Gulf Coast fractionators and the LPG export terminal JV with ONEOK come online. When this happens, the wellhead-to-water slogan becomes an asset base that is vertically integrated and next to impossible to replicate.

I think that MPLX’s distribution yield and valuation look enticing at these levels

MPLX is paying a distribution of $4.31, which is a 7.71% distribution yield. I believe that MPLX is in a unique spot as it's supporting the 2nd highest yield among the large-cap midstream names, which include Energy Transfer (ET), Enterprise Products Partners (EPD), ONEOK (OKE), and Kinder Morgan (KMI), with the highest distribution growth commitment of 12.5% in 2026 and 2027. MPLX’s distribution coverage ratio of 1.3x supports their distribution growth plan, and when I do the math and apply 12.5% annualized growth over the next 2 years, the distribution would increase by $1.15, or 26.56%, over the next 2 years to $5.45. I think that the combination of yield and distribution growth is an incredible asset for income investors that are not only looking for stable income but income that can grow more than the rate of inflation.

When I compare MPLX to an extended peer group, I am confident in what I am seeing. MPLX trades at 7.79 times EBITDA compared to the peer group average of 7.60 times and 9.62 times operating cash flow compared to a peer group average of 9.4 times. MPLX is slightly higher than the peer group on these items, but with all of the projects coming online, I think that they can bring these multiples significantly lower. On an FCF methodology, MPLX trades at 13.86 times, and the peer group average is 27.73 times. The reality is that MPLX trades at the 2nd lowest valuation on FCF, and most of the peer group trades at more than 20 times FCF. From a valuation standpoint, I think that MPLX looks interesting again, especially with the large distribution yield exceeding 7.5%.

The risks of investing in MPLX

While I am bullish again on MPLX, there are several risk factors to consider. Permian producer activity is the primary risk, and MPLX’s 2026 organic plan assumes that production in the Permian remains elevated for an extended period. If natural gas prices stay volatile or if oil pulls back materially, drilling activity could slow down dramatically. This would impact the returns from Secretariat II and the Northwind assets, which would impact the future cash flow for MPLX. MPLX also ended 2025 with a leverage ratio of 3.7x, and if they decide to increase borrowings without their cash flow expanding at the same rate, it could negatively impact the way some investors look at MPLX. Investors should do their own due diligence because there could also be technological breakthroughs in the future that disrupt traditional energy sources, and if small nuclear reactors become more popular, big tech companies could opt for this technology rather than contracting for natural gas-fired power plants to power their facilities.

Conclusion

I think MPLX offers the clearest multi-year setup in the large-cap midstream space right now, as all of the pieces should come together in 2026. MPLX has a distribution yield of 7.71%, with the distribution growing at 12.5% and a $2.4 billion 2026 growth budget designed around Permian-to-Gulf Coast gas and NGL infrastructure at exactly the moment U.S. LNG exports are scaling to backfill Qatar and replace Russian gas in Europe. I’m upgrading my investment thesis to bullish from neutral as I believe that 2026 will become a commissioning year and the full force of these assets will be felt in the financials during the 2027 fiscal year. I think that investors are able to buy a great multi-year setup for MPLX at these levels as the wellhead-to-water structure starts printing more cash flow for MPLX.

data.title: Farmland Partners: Remains Problematic At This Time
data.description:
Farmland and Timber REITs are not in any way a bad business idea overall - it's a relatively good investment idea. The combination of an essential asset, like the type of land or ground that this represents (which is an appreciating asset), with an inflation-hedged upside technically should allow a very attractive combination of income - leases and timber income - together without requiring the increased complexity and risk of direct ownership of these assets. At least that is the argument.

As farmland represents an asset that should appreciate as food prices increase, this should lead (technically) to higher income from the asset, in turn increasing the value of the land. This also creates a very nice source of income, and the fact that we're talking about biological growth means we don't need a bank or anyone else to provide a "compound interest" - the land itself, through Mother Nature, provides this compounding. Farmland yields and vegetables/fruits grow even in a struggling economy. This is an asset class that is nicely correlated to a long-term sort of demand, and because it's a finite resource, meaning scarcity at a point in the future.

So why doesn't it work out? FPI, which is the subject of today's article, has been underperforming the average for quite some time. While seeing some recent surge, the historical trends for the company are not favorable. FPI has generated negative price appreciation despite good AFFO growth and has underperformed the SPY on every single comparison - from the 1-year average down to the 20-year annualized average, which is now at 2.37%, compared to the 9.57% when looking at the SPY.

There are some very clear reasons as to why timber and farmland assets are underperforming, and also some reasons that need a bit more explanation. In this article, I'll try to address both of these categories and also cover what might get the company out of its slump (but also, why it's in fact rather tricky, with farmland a bit trickier than Timber).

Farmland Partners - Upside Would Require More Than Just Sentiment

Aside from the company's relatively low leverage and good business idea, the arguments for why FPI is a good or resilient business are actually quite few. The company is hounded by earnings forecast misses, which now make up the majority of its forecasting/accuracy history.

The fact that the company typically trades below its NAV is a good indication of its overall earnings or forecast, or lack thereof. But there are also other reasons for why the company is underperforming. The falling commodity prices of US farmland are a core reason why the company and why farmland prices are dropping. Due to both tariffs as well as other reasons, the company is seeing a clear decline in prices not only for soybeans, which were primarily a Chinese export (now Brazil is handling much of this), but also for corn. This decline came at the end of last year and has persisted until now, which has pressured operating and EBIT margins for farmland and farms.

While this may not have a direct impact on company earnings, which are stable due to the contract structure, the impacts on the NAV/valuation for the company's underlying assets are undeniable. If these low valuations persist, there's also a real risk that there may be a decline in rental payments or that the company may be forced to renegotiate. Farmland assets also share other correlations that impact earnings, such as climate events. Not only events like wildfires and hurricanes, but also pests and other factors, such as droughts.

All of these things have a direct impact on this company's earnings.

The company is primarily a play on macro arguments. Macro arguments like the ones you see above - the GDP/Capita arguments and the population relative to farmland and arable land. These sorts of arguments are very hard to discount because they are (in my view) true, but they are also extremely hard to value. How do we put into a dollar amount the decline in arable land on a per-person basis? How then, if we were to do this, do we put it into a valuation that makes sense to this company?

I do not believe anyone can easily come up with such a formula, and the company's earnings misses show me that the company is used to being overestimated by the market by virtue of its macro arguments. This, by the way, is despite this company being one of the most significant plays on farmland in all of the US (publicly traded at least), despite what I would also consider a relatively limited market capitalization of sub-$550M at today's levels.

The company has further been impacted by some one-off items, which I wrote about in my last piece, which are likely to severely impact earnings in the near term. There's the special dividend and the portfolio simplification in reducing its overall size. I still consider the brokerage business sale to be a mistake due to the conservative, fee-based income that the asset provided, which was also decorrelated from farmland trends. The simplification done by this asset sale has turned FPI into a completely correlated farmland asset stock.

The upside that some investors expect for FPI is based on the notion that the company is going to see interest rate cuts in 2026. I view the likelihood of such cuts to be low. Without interest rate cuts and the reduction in capital costs that follow, the farmland assets and the REIT in question are unlikely to see significant materialization of upside. The current estimate for the 2026E period is for AFFO to reduce to about $0.32/share, which would represent an 18% drop and a reduction back to nearly 2024 levels, erasing much of the 2025 upside that also drove the share price outperformance. It's very important for me to say that the results we've seen as of late don't even come close to matching what the company saw in 2015-2016, its peak, when the company generated nearly $0.6/share in AFFO, almost twice the amount we see today. At the time, FPI merged with AFCO and increased diversification, which increased specialty crops.

The reasons for decline since then are multifaceted, but they do include lower pricing for commodities that the company is dependent on, rising interest rates with higher debt, and asset-selling strategies. So the company's strategy of offloading non-core assets to pay down debt is not a new idea by FPI, and it has been pressuring AFFO for going on 10 years at this time.

So where is FPI likely to go from here?

The recent results and trends at the company following my article were good. It included a dividend boost of 50%, which also served its role in creating a significant push for the share price. We now have a yield of over 4.7%, which outperforms the risk-free rate. But I consider the recent results given to us in February to be tricky. To me, it seems that its AFFO estimates are reliant on one-time gains from sales in a market that doesn't seem all that favorable in terms of offloading these types of assets. Also, as the company continues to sell off revenue-generating assets, the asset base for the company is obviously shrinking significantly. It's also not as though crop price volatility is on the decline - rather it's on the rise, especially where the company is selling assets, as in California.

I view one of the main threats to the company as its continued shrinkage of the asset base. This creates a reverse efficiency (or inefficiency) problem. You see, as the company's income base shrinks, the company still needs a base level of G&A and CapEx. As the revenues shrink, these expenses become an ever-increasing amount of the company's total revenues - and as the potential softness from valuation increases for the asset base, the company grows more and more uncertain in terms of fair value.

Let me show you what I mean.

Farmland Partners - Valuation is Not Favorable

To call the valuation favorable here would take a strong stomach. Premiumization is required. To use the company's 10-14 year average, Farmland Partners is at about 35-40x P/AFFO - far higher than anything I'd be comfortable with, especially with a $500M market capitalization.

I last put my target at about $9.8/share. That's about 15%+ below the current share price valuation. As of this article, I'm lowering that PT to $9/share. Let me explain why.

The company seems very dead-set on its journey to divest assets under the name of non-core and efficiencies. I view all of these divestments as uncertain at best and inefficiency-creating at worst. My $9/share represents a significant discount to NAV, but I also argue that the company has never proven that this is where farmland assets are to be accurately valued. By valuing it at $9/share, I believe that I am focusing on the land value itself while also taking into account that Farmland may increase my value by buying back its own shares for some of the sales proceeds.

The $9/share target also completely aligns with my interest rate and commodity pricing estimate. By discounting at what amounts to less than 35x P/AFFO, I'm saying that I expect a high-interest rate (or at least, no cuts) with a low commodity attractiveness environment. Given the current geopolitical macro, I believe this to be entirely correct - I think commodity prices are going down here, or at least not going up. I do not believe this is the time to create a situation where inefficiencies are entirely possible. Clearly, I am also not the only one who believes this, given the slew of recent downgrades the company has been the subject of.

I cut my PT to $9/share and hold my rating of "HOLD" here, with the following main risks and considerations.

Risks & Considerations

The main risks for this company are the size, the uncertainty about valuation levels and where the company is "fairly" valued, and the exposure to volatile commodity prices. The current protectionism and tariff situation does not make things better for FPI, in particular when considering where the competition for US crops comes from. Many US farmers sell their soybeans to China, and the market access here has been substantially reduced by tariffs and China's own move to sourcing from places like Brazil. The US was once a dominant exporter of this, and this has left farmers with not only soybeans but also costs.

The dynamics in this REIT are more volatile than management seems to want to realize or to market. Because of this and because of the company's risks from its size, where CapEx and overhead costs are a substantial part of revenues, I consider the risks to be more elevated than in traditional REITs. To that come the risks mentioned of downsizing, which I consider to be the entirely wrong time to do so here.

Thesis

Farmland is typically discounted below its net asset value. This leaves it to the appeal of the company and, specifically, its operational leverage and potential to see how attractive investing in it could be. Farmland Partners is, unfortunately, a REIT where I believe that the only way to safely invest is by substantially discounting the NAV to account for the high rate of costs that the company, due to its small size, has.

As of my introductory article, I would be interested in investing in the company at about $9/share, which represents a roughly 30% discounted rate to its net asset value, adjusted for the company's asset rotation plans. At that price, I might be interested, but there are still fundamental risks to US farming that need to be looked at.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (in italics).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside that is high enough, based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators and 0 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 3/5 "stars" here, which typically isn't enough. Based on this and a price target of $9/share, I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me. I give the company a "Hold" rating as of April 2026.

data.title: Autoliv: Airbags, Buybacks, And A Reasonable Multiple
data.description:
Autoliv (ALV) outperformed expectations when it reported first-quarter results. This is a company that investors tend to group with other automotive stocks, assigning low valuation multiples. Despite operating in a tough industry, Autoliv has shown it has at least a narrow competitive moat. This is reflected in an elevated market share, relatively high profit margins, and pricing power. In other words, it is a good company in a tough industry.

The environment in which it operates still has an impact on Autoliv, which limits its upside potential. Light vehicle production (LVP) is forecast to decline 1% this year, and the customer base is highly concentrated, somewhat reducing Autoliv's pricing power. Autoliv has been able to outperform the market thanks to a few key factors. Safety content per vehicle continues to increase, enabling Autoliv to grow faster than global LVP. Autoliv also has scale and intellectual property advantages that make it difficult for rivals to take business away. These advantages also provide pricing power, as discussed the last time we covered ALV. The ability to pass on most of the higher material and tariff costs has protected profits.

Financials

Because auto purchases are highly discretionary, Autoliv's financials reflect the cyclicality of its end-market. Despite occasional declines, over the long -term, Autoliv has managed to deliver respectable revenue growth of close to 6% per year. Operating margin has been helped by proactive efficiency measures taken by management. These included moving some of the R&D to lower-cost regions, accelerating factory automation initiatives, and streamlining administrative functions.

Autoliv is likely to remain a business with embedded cyclicality. What is changing is that by widening the operating margin, the company has become more resilient to downturns. Even with a less-than-ideal automotive market, Autoliv is delivering operating margins near record levels, and has continued to grow earnings per share (EPS).

The transformation from a decent business, to a good one, is also reflected in the elevated return on capital employed (ROCE). For comparison, automotive suppliers BorgWarner (BWA) and Lear Corp. (LEA) currently have ROCEs of roughly 12%, while Toyota Motor (TM), the largest auto manufacturer in the world, has a ROCE of close to 6.6%. Autoliv has managed to be a high-quality business in a very tough industry.

Research & Development

Autoliv continues to expand its already vast intellectual property portfolio, with thousands of granted patents, and thousands more in pending status. Many of these patents focus on small improvements to make the core products, such as airbags, seat belts, and steering wheels, safer or more comfortable.

There are also efforts to expand to adjacent areas, and we saw a clear example on the first quarter earnings call. Autoliv introduced the first commercially ready airbag for motorcycles as part of its efforts to expand beyond its core business. Autoliv is targeting 1% to 2% growth from Mobility Safety Solutions, and this is a start, but unlikely to generate significant revenue in the short term. Responding to an analyst question, CEO Mikael Bratt emphasized that adjacent areas are likely to be impactful by 2030, saying, "the 4% to 6% and the 1% to 2% LVP, 1% to 2% content, and the 1% to 2% coming from Mobility Safety Solutions should come through towards the end of this period here, which we mean 2030, before it becomes meaningful."

Pockets of Growth

Management can also be given credit for positioning the company well to capitalize on the few pockets of growth in the industry. During the earnings call, management cited China, India, and South Korea as being markets still seeing strong growth. India, in particular, delivered impressive organic sales growth of 38% year-over-year for Autoliv. This was possible thanks to high LVP growth in the country and also regulatory and consumer demand for higher safety in vehicles. In three years, India has gone from representing 2% of Autoliv's sales to roughly 6% and continues to grow at a rapid rate.

Balance Sheet

Autoliv has been disciplined with capital allocation, avoiding expensive acquisitions or overexpansion. Instead, Autoliv has leaned more towards joint ventures and collaboration to gain access to new technologies in a capital-efficient manner. This has protected the balance sheet and helped maintain low leverage. Autoliv's net debt-to-EBITDA ratio increased from 1.1x to 1.3x during the first quarter, but still remains below its 1.5x target.

Shareholder Returns

A solid balance sheet, combined with healthy free cash flow generation, has allowed Autoliv to deliver attractive shareholder returns through dividends and repurchases. The shareholder yield, which combines the dividend and buyback yields, is roughly 6.5% at the moment. This means the business does not have to grow much to deliver double-digit total returns for investors. Even mid-single-digit revenue and profit growth could potentially result in total returns exceeding 10% long-term.

Future Outlook

The outlook remains mixed for the company, and the automotive industry in general. Autoliv's guidance is based on an estimate of global LVP decreasing roughly 1% in 2026. This should result in organic sales remaining roughly flat for the year. Earnings per share can still grow modestly, as a result of continued share buybacks and a slight improvement in the company's operating margin. For context, Autoliv is currently aiming for between $300 million and $500 million in buybacks through 2029. This is significant given Autoliv's current market capitalization of close to $9 billion.

Looking further ahead, management has talked about its ambition to reach 4% to 6% annual revenue growth. To get there, they would need LVP growth of 1% to 2%, which could be somewhat optimistic given the current negative growth. The 1% to 2% expansion in safety content per vehicle has been more reliable in recent quarters. The remaining 1% to 2% would come from expanding to adjacent markets, like motorcycle safety. This target appears achievable, even if it is likely to take time to accomplish.

Valuation

Perhaps one of the strongest points in favor of the shares is the undemanding valuation. Autoliv is likely to continue delivering weak revenue growth, but a Price/Earnings multiple of roughly 12x already reflects this reality.

What is also important is that a large percentage of the earnings are being distributed back to shareholders through share repurchases and dividends. Margins have been expanding through efficiency measures, ROCE has improved, and the balance sheet is strong. Despite Autoliv arguably being a higher quality business today, shares currently trade below their ten-year valuation multiples.

Risks

The main risk we see with Autoliv is the cyclicality of the automotive industry. Interest rates, credit availability, fuel prices, and consumer sentiment can significantly lower demand. This is why the current environment is challenging for Autoliv. Interest rates in the U.S. have increased considerably, consumer sentiment is extremely low, and higher default rates mean lenders will tighten standards.

Another important risk is a quality issue that could result in defective products. Given that these are products to protect passenger safety, the company has to be extremely careful to avoid defects.

Takeaway

Autoliv delivered a solid quarter in what remains a challenging environment for companies in the auto sector. Even if headwinds start to dissipate, it is unlikely that Autoliv will become a high-growth company. Still, investors should not be too quick to dismiss it. Autoliv continues to make progress with its efficiency measures to improve operating margins; it is well positioned in the markets that still have rapid growth, such as China and India, and safety content per vehicle continues to increase.

At current valuation multiples, and with a shareholder yield of close to 6.5%, even modest growth can result in total returns exceeding 10% for long-term investors. Expansion to motorcycle and wearable safety shows the company continues to pursue new growth opportunities, while remaining disciplined with its capital allocation. We therefore continue to rate shares as a "Buy", though investors should be conscious of the headwinds in the industry.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: British American Tobacco: Why This 6% Yield Remains My Largest Holding
data.description:
It has been a while since I last covered my largest holding by far, British American Tobacco (BTI), to which I'm going to refer as BAT going forward.

For those who didn't follow my previous coverage, I bought the stock at the height of the tobacco bear market a couple of years ago, when the shares offered close to double-digit dividend yields. With the rising share price and valuation, I have downgraded the rating from "Strong Buy" to "Buy".

Momentum investors might disagree with me here, but in my opinion, that's the right approach, especially when it comes to low-growth businesses like tobacco companies. We have to be bullish when prices are low and dividend yields are high, and we become more cautious once the valuation rises.

Whenever investing in "boring" consumer staples, the dividend is a major factor in total returns over the very long term. Naturally, we want our starting yield to be high and the valuation to be low. That way, we not only profit from juicy dividend yields but also from potential multiple expansion.

And that's exactly what happened with British American Tobacco. Total returns have been staggering from its bear market lows, even outpacing the NASDAQ.

But the past is the past, so where do we stand now?

After the correction over the last few weeks, BAT is once again trading at a 6% dividend yield and a P/E ratio of 11.69. It still offers a margin of safety, even though the shares have risen by ~100% from their bear market lows! That's how undervalued the company really was.

My estimate of BAT's intrinsic value is around 14 to 15 times earnings. At that valuation, the shares would yield around 4.6% to 5%. Add conservative low-to-mid-single-digit growth in EPS and dividends, and we'd still arrive at a return that's in line with the markets over the long term.

But here's an important point that's often forgotten in today's exuberant markets: as investors, we don't want to pay fair value; we need to require a margin of safety.

Right now, shares are trading at a 20% discount to my estimate of intrinsic value. It's a large enough safety buffer to issue a "Buy" rating, especially if we consider the resiliency of the tobacco market.

Tobacco Stocks: The Forgotten Safe Haven

In a world where markets are rising from one all-time high to the next, fewer people want to invest in boring but relatively safe companies growing in the single digits. It's only when greed turns into fear, that dividend stocks with reliable cash flows could become the next highfliers. I believe the world is still cyclical, so it's not a matter of if it will happen, but when.

Tobacco stocks, like BAT, have historically been some of the most reliable dividend stocks one could own, despite falling cigarette volumes over the decades.

The secret lies in the weird economics of the tobacco industry. A 10% price hike does not translate into a 10% price increase at retail because of the high government taxes on cigarettes. I described this phenomenon in previous articles like this one.

Also, companies in the sector have some of the highest gross margins of any consumer product. BAT's gross margin is 83%—well above Coca-Cola's 62% gross margin, which is also best in class.

A high gross margin makes BAT less affected by rising input costs. The company can weather weaker demand or inflationary pressures much better than most companies. And it shows already.

Tobacco Companies vs. Consumer Staple Peers

The last two years have not been a great time for many consumer staple peers. A demand slump and rising costs have weighed on the margins and profitability of food and alcohol companies, but tobacco is bucking the trend.

Not only did the share price rise at a time when many peers were struggling, but the underlying profitability remains intact. One could argue that tobacco companies are still some of the most resilient businesses out there. And not too long ago, one could've bought the whole sector at single-digit P/FCF ratios. If that's not a sign that Mr. Market isn't always right, then I don't know what is.

Next-Generation Products

A couple of weeks ago, I downgraded Imperial Brands from a "Buy" to a "Hold" based on valuation concerns alone. It's trading at similar valuation levels as BAT, so why am I more optimistic about the latter? The answer lies in Next-Generation Products (NGP).

While I do expect the tobacco segment to remain resilient over the next decade, as a long-term investor, I tend to think in decades. NGPs are the future of nicotine consumption, and in order to avoid their "Kodak Moment", tobacco companies have to invest in them.

Given its status as a smaller player in the industry with limited ambition to dominate, I have very little hope for Imperial Brands. BAT's NGP portfolio, on the other hand, looks quite promising. I believe the chances are rising that over the long term, BAT could thrive in a world without cigarettes.

The company is constantly innovating at a faster pace in all categories, and with some patience, I believe it might get there. When I started investing in the company, NGPs were 5.6% of total revenues; five years later, they are 14.13%. And if we add the traditional oral segment, total smokeless revenues are 18.20% of revenues.

NGPs turned profitable two years ago, and since then, profits have been growing at a rapid pace. Let's quickly go through the different categories, starting with the most promising one, modern oral.

Modern Oral

For a long time, modern oral had been a double-edged sword for BAT. It was a rapidly growing category in Europe, but the U.S. had been lagging quite substantially. The reason was that BAT had to offer an inferior product in the U.S. due to regulations. All that changed with the release of Velo Plus in December 2024.

Now, the company has arguably the best product in the U.S. market, and it shows in the numbers. I had been predicting this outperformance long before the results finally proved it.

In 2025, Velo Plus ended the year with a 24% volume share, up from just 6.1% at the beginning of the year. Modern oral sales in the U.S. grew by 297%. In the first half of the year, 1.1 billion pouches were sold, and in the second half, 2.4 billion. Sales more than doubled in just half a year!

Velo Plus is a wet pouch with stable nicotine delivery, and most consumers prefer it to the dry pouches of the market-leading ZYN. BAT is now the global market leader in the fastest-growing and arguably most-promising NGP category.

The company expects revenues to grow from the current £1.2 billion to £4.3 billion in 2030. That'd result in a CAGR of 29.08%. This is an ambitious target, but I believe it can be done as long as the competitive pressures from smaller players don't significantly increase from here.

After all, it's a relatively new category, and the segment comes with high gross margins. It doesn't cost much to produce a can of nicotine pouches, and the barriers to entry are relatively low, but it requires expertise to produce the most satisfying product out there. BAT has a century of experience and studies when it comes to nicotine delivery.

There are plenty of smaller brands, but none of them quite "hit" like Velo, according to my own research. Also, as these categories mature, I expect more regulations around the world, which on average should benefit larger players with expertise like BAT and Philip Morris. After all, these companies have decades of experience in highly regulated markets, and they have the financial resources to handle them.

Heated Tobacco

As the largest holding in my personal portfolio, I wish I could report only good news on BAT, but in order to judge the long-term potential of the company, we need to stay objective.

The new heated tobacco devices, glo Hilo and Hilo Plus, have been introduced to several markets in H2 2025. In a previous article, I was skeptical about the potential of these new devices, even though in many regards they offer better features than the market leader, IQOS. So far, it seems that my fears have materialized.

The devices have been out in large markets like Japan, Italy, and Poland, but there is not much chatter about them on social media. When they are discussed, the opinions are mixed. That's not a good sign. It's safe to say glo Hilo hasn't gone viral yet.

What further makes me even more cautious is that data from online retailers like Amazon shows that glo Hilo doesn't sell well. On Amazon Italy, for example, glo Hilo only sold 50 devices over the last month. Italy is one of the largest heated tobacco markets globally.

Both devices seem to be a great improvement from previous generations, but BAT has raised the price of its sticks to IQOS levels. It seems like most consumers are sticking to the market leader, as a cheaper price was the main attraction for using glo.

At this point, it's safe to say that IQOS has built a moat. And if we think about it, it makes sense. If you're a heated tobacco user, the most important thing is wide availability. You want to buy your sticks at every gas station, every convenience store, and every grocery market. Not only that, you want your favorite flavor to be available.

As a former smoker, I can confirm that the worst thing that could happen to a nicotine user is running out of nicotine. As the market leader with over 80% market share, IQOS is more widely available than glo. It's a moat that's hard to break into, and it requires a massively better product to convince IQOS users to switch to glo.

At the pace BAT is innovating, I believe the company might get there, but patience is required. It could take at least one more generation before we see the heated tobacco segment pick up.

Vapour

While heated tobacco is shielded from competition outside Big Tobacco, vaping is not. Here, the company is not only competing against Philip Morris but, more importantly, against hundreds of Chinese companies, which in many cases sell illicit products.

It's not a level playing field, and as long as the Chinese competition is offering huge vapes in all kinds of flavors at very little cost, I don't see BAT winning in this category. The Chinese competition is not solely a phenomenon in the U.S., but is globally present. The only way for BAT to win in the vapour segment is effective and enforced regulation. Over the very long term, I'm quite confident we're going to see exactly that.

The U.S. is the most important market for vaping, and in H2 2025, we saw the early signs of increased regulation and enforcement. Vuse has returned to growth in the U.S., which is an encouraging early sign.

The main reasons for it are stronger federal and state-level enforcement, but the environment remains volatile.

Outside the U.S., the company launched Vuse Ultra, a smart vaping device with a built-in display and a customizable experience. I believe it's an improvement from the previous generation, but at least in Europe and Canada, the competitive pressures from Chinese companies will likely continue weighing on sales.

Since its launch, there has been very little chatter about the Vuse Ultra online, and the sales on platforms like Amazon don't look promising. We need more regulation and enforcement in order for BAT to thrive in the vaping category globally; innovation is not enough. The U.S. has seen a promising start, but the rest of the world has to catch up.

A Few Last Words on NGPs

It's easy to get lost in all the developments concerning NGPs. It's a fast-changing and volatile environment. The most important thing is to keep winning in the fastest-growing and arguably most profitable (on gross margins) category going forward, and that is modern oral. BAT's modern oral business is firing on all cylinders. Not only in Europe, but more importantly also in the U.S., the largest nicotine market globally.

Another important fact is that almost all profits are still generated by combustibles. It's a category that's shielded from outside competition due to regulations. It's proven to deliver stable cash flows despite a long history of falling volumes.

A few setbacks in heated tobacco and vaping do not threaten the investment thesis. Over the long term, BAT has the potential to do well in all NGP categories, but patience is required. The 6% dividend yield at current levels sweetens the waiting time.

Risk Factors

BAT is operating in an industry with falling volumes; if the decline accelerates from here, it could hurt cash flows. Nicotine is highly regulated around the world, and new regulations could lead to lower profitability.

NGPs are the future of the nicotine industry, and the shift toward smokeless tobacco has opened the door for competition. Modern oral and especially vaping are highly fragmented markets, and success in these new categories over the long term is not certain.

Conclusion

BAT's modern oral business is accelerating, and the tobacco cash cow keeps funding buybacks, dividends, and investments in NGPs. BAT has been very resilient at a time when many other consumer staple peers are struggling with shrinking margins and falling volumes.

I perceive BAT to be a recession-resistant business that's increasingly future-proofing its business model. The path is not without setbacks, but over the long term, success is likely.

At a 11.69x P/E, the company is not as cheap as it once was; therefore, I'm not as bullish as I once was. At a 20% discount to its intrinsic value, the shares remain a "Buy", but the air is getting thinner. In order to achieve great total returns with low-growth companies, the valuation paid is of utmost importance.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: The Year's Most Positive Signal Rallies Markets, But Iran Risk Favors Select Sectors
data.description:
Introduction

The vertical line on a chart is almost always followed by a vertical line in the other direction. Speed kills in both directions." ~ Paul Tudor Jones

The most dangerous thing is to buy something at the peak of its popularity. At that point, all the favorable facts and optimistic interpretations have been built into the price." ~ Howard Marks

Timing matters, and it matters greatly. I have spent the last 35 years trading, researching, and constructing algorithms to identify and leverage the value across fundamental, technical, and behavioral finance models. Of the ten portfolio models designed for optimal portfolio mixes for members to beat the market at Value & Momentum Breakouts, eight come from enhancing well-tested anomaly research in published financial journals. All of the models continue to outperform the S&P 500 in live forward testing here on Seeking Alpha, and again this year.

Whether you are into dividends, ETFs, long-term value, forensic accounting anomalies, CFO insider trades, or aggressive short-term breakout picks, most of what I do is point investors to opportunities here on Seeking Alpha for large short- and long-term gains with a long track record of success based on your risk preferences.

The Year's Most Positive Signal Rallies Markets, But Iran Risk Favors Select Sectors

Our S&P 500 momentum gauges reflect the largest stocks and fund flows in the market and react to the MDA [multiple-discriminant analysis] cycle conditions of all 500 stocks in the index. This ratio of the most extreme positive (Segment 6) and extreme negative (Segment 2) gives us the momentum signal changes daily, weekly, and monthly. The weekly S&P 500 gauges below show the most extreme positive reversal in the multi-year data set, from near max positive lows on March 30th to current highs of positive momentum back to March 28, 2024.

In my March market article, I discussed the best signs for confirming early market bottoms as we approached peak negative MG values in March. Dip-buyers had been adding through the longest negative signal and 5 consecutive S&P 500 weeks of declines since 2022 until a huge capitulation at the end of March:

  • Dip-Buyers Ride Longest Negative Signal Since 2022 To Next Tactical Bottom

As expected, peak negative momentum reversed from the highest negative levels since April 2025. But, unexpectedly, this reversal has been the fastest frenzied rush to return capital to the market in many years. It appears the primary catalyst has been picking the end of the Iran war, now with an unstable and uncertain ceasefire pause between the US and Iran.

This news-driven market cycle began its incredible rally the hour President Trump remarked publicly that "the Iran war may end within 48 hours." That fueled a +2.91% rally in the S&P 500 on March 31st and the largest 3-week market gain in over a year after suffering five consecutive weeks of declines. A few days later, Iran shot down a US fighter jet and rejected any talks for peace. The market moved ever higher in bullish stair-step patterns, and the current ceasefire pause has sustained the record gains so far into April. What comes next is likely more volatility and risk that favors certain trades and certain sectors.

Ordinarily I talk about how to detect the strongest early market bottom using only Sector and Market gauges, as I shared last time:

1. The S&P 500 and Technology gauges turning positive again.

2. A majority of sector gauges turning positive, including a majority of the 3 mega-cap sectors, Communications, Technology, and Consumer Cyclicals.

3. At least 2 consecutive days of market gains and three consecutive days of rising positive gauge values.

Or you could watch how these different gauges come off near bottoms for a rebound into Q2.

Just a few short weeks ago we were talking in our investment group about max negative momentum conditions with all the gauges negative to an extreme, except Energy and the likelihood of some rebound into Q2:

  • Echoes Of 2022: Bear Bounces As ETF Signal Portfolio Leads S&P 500 By Over 23%

Now I am already discussing the risks of market tops in this incredible rush to benefit from the lows caused by record-high Oil prices and the Iran war. This has easily been the fastest reversal from low to high momentum that I can document in more than 5 years.

What Are the Opportunities?

As I say frequently in prior articles, "We can never have a Major Index recovery without the participation of the semiconductor segment." Our market indices are most heavily weighted on mega-cap stocks. Mega-cap stocks are most numerous in the technology sector, and that is most impacted by the semiconductor segment.

The two biggest movers right now are Technology and Energy with high divergence and potential for more large reversals.

The Direxion Daily Semiconductor Bull 3X ETF (SOXL) chart shows the extreme reaction as April began.

As I have highlighted above, the technical indicators are back in high short-term overbought levels that have always been followed with some level of profit-taking.

What makes the Technology sector signal unique is that we have 99 Semiconductor-related stocks in the sector, while we have 395 Software-related stocks, and their returns this year are extremely divided. 15% of the Semiconductors have over $100 billion market cap, while only 2.7% of the Software sector are $100 billion or larger. This contributes to an ongoing skew where fewer larger cap stocks are pushing the market higher while more smaller technology stocks are heading lower.

Obviously the Market index weightings follow the market sizes, with semiconductor giant NVIDIA (NVDA) growing to over $4.9 trillion in market cap now. This is a larger market cap than all the small-cap Russell 2000 stocks combined and more than 30% larger. NVIDIA has become a "Market" index of its own and remains highly positive into April until the next momentum signal change.

Meanwhile, on the software side of the technology sector, we have many stocks languishing near prior-year lows.

State Street SPDR S&P 500 Software & Services ETF (XSW) is suffering another large correction with strong similarities back to 2022 and 2024. The current software selloff is largely attributed to the AI takeover benefiting the chip manufacturers in the same technology sector.

So now you have some idea of the market skew in the technology sector. A majority of the more numerous and smaller-cap software stocks are below their 200-day moving averages at a 3.4:1 ratio, while quite a large majority 2.5:1 of the Semiconductors larger-cap stocks are well above their 200-day moving average.

So How Should We Trade the Technology Sector?

Longer term, the smaller cap software stocks look to have more growth potential in their recent MDA breakout signals.

For example, SoundHound (SOUN) in the software application segment has triggered a positive MDA buy signal across more than 75 tested variables for the first time in 2026. This selection comes from our actively traded Premium portfolio that is up +16.68% YTD trading in 45 fewer trading days than the S&P 500 (SPX) by following the gauge signals shown above.

Additionally, we hold Micron Technology (MU) among the mega-cap semiconductors, watching for another potentially sharp reversal in the same actively traded portfolio. You can see the enormous difference between the two momentum charts of SoundHound AI and Micron Technology.

What Are the Risks That Are Occurring?

Obviously we have the Iran war risk that is adversely impacting oil prices and creating some remarkable market similarities to 2022. I have written about this previously, when energy was the best and only positive-performing sector for the year. We also had a serious financial meltdown that year among some riskier banks. This post below from last month was right before the S&P 500 tested the low of 6353 at the end of March.

Those risks are definitely getting the most attention. However, we have a looming risk of extreme market skew, somewhat discussed in the Technology divergence between small caps and large caps.

S&P 500 Equal vs. Market-Cap Weighted Record Skew

Throughout 2024 I highlighted this chart of the mega skew with concerns and optimism that we would soon break out to a broad rally condition and away from an extreme concentration in the largest mega-cap stocks.

More than a year later, as we enter the second quarter of 2026, we are seeing the skew accelerate again into many of the very largest S&P 500 stocks at a rate that moves the chart below the 1.20 level and back to risks of more large-cap declines like Q1. We certainly could be on the cusp of a small-cap, financial sector, and broad market recovery longer term that produced rapid unwinding of this skew in 2008 and 2020, but not yet.

This leads me back to one of my 2026 forecast articles on Seeking Alpha about the new Fed Chairman and large potential for out-of-favor value stocks to do extremely well.

  • New Fed Chair, QE & Lower Rates: Where The 2026 Piotroski-Graham Value Stocks Are Hiding

Back then Kevin Warsh only had 35% odds of getting nominated for Fed Chairman, but now it appears nearly a foregone conclusion that he will replace Chairman Powell near the end of May. The projected impact may be the same as I was forecasting, with additional rate cuts ahead of us regardless of market conditions by the May time frame.

So Are the Prior Value Stocks Still Good?

The current January Piotroski-Graham value portfolio is up +13.0% YTD in our tracking from January. Adjusted for large dividends, the Piotroski portfolio is up +14.2%, with the Dividend portfolio up +11.5% YTD

These long-term value stocks are intended for 2-year buy/hold measurements from the scholars who originally published the algorithms in the financial literature. One of the Piotroski value stocks I highlighted in my last article in March was, at the time, the worst-performing value stock from January in this long-term portfolio:

  • CFO Insiders Leading The Markets As Value And Dividends Beat Growth

Since then, Harley-Davidson (HOG) has been on a rapid breakout even as the CFO insider anomaly stocks are accelerating through earnings season. Many of us know well from prior studies that CFOs clearly know when their company stock is a good value and when to load up without creating undue attention.

The Harley-Davidson value selection from the January Piotroski-Graham value portfolio went from the worst-performing stock in the portfolio to now up over +12.8% YTD. The best part is that these value selections from well-documented, peer-reviewed algorithms have consistently delivered long-term gains through very challenging markets, as live-tested here on Seeking Alpha for more than 9 years.

In this long-term value portfolio, we leverage published financial algorithms from scholars like Benjamin Graham and Joseph Piotroski, who have well-documented results in the literature of beating the markets across different decades in their proven research. Without going into all the details and prior computational articles, I have combined their algorithms and enhanced them further to release these Piotroski-Graham value portfolios two times a year, in July and January.

Energy is the Biggest Mover With Iran Uncertainty

The Energy sector gauges, most heavily weighted on oil and natural gas, are finally turning negative after 13 weeks positive from the lows last year. As in prior negative sector signals like January 2025, the decline is unlikely to be straight down to new lows but so far has been the most profitable sector trade in our active ETF portfolios, both in bull/bear funds YTD.

Prior to the signal changes two weeks ago, the Energy sector bull funds were up over 78% in Direxion Daily Energy Bull 2X ETF (ERX), shown in yellow on the chart above, and up +35% in State Street Energy Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLE). Now we are riding the negative signals in Energy bear funds that are likely on a path toward longer-term equilibrium in the $70/bbl range.

FINRA cautions investors that:

Exchange-traded funds (ETFs) that offer leverage or that are designed to perform inversely to the index or benchmark they track, or both, are growing in number and popularity. While such products may be useful in some sophisticated trading strategies, they are highly complex financial instruments that are typically designed to achieve their stated objectives on a daily basis. Due to the effects of compounding, their performance over longer periods of time can differ significantly from their stated daily objective. Therefore, inverse and leveraged ETFs that are reset daily typically are unsuitable for retail investors who plan to hold them for longer than one trading session, particularly in volatile markets.

Conclusion

What I offer readers is intended to share different financial models for your future success. We all know that timing matters greatly. Scholars have documented for years that "harvesting different risk baskets, each with a premium that has low-to-negative correlations to the others," is an excellent way to produce strong risk-adjusted returns. These ongoing sector rotations and signal changes continue to give us many new opportunities for profit in the years ahead as we build our optimal portfolio mixes each year.

I always encourage considering three steps in stock selection, whether for long-term value or short-term gains:

  1. Check the direction of the Market Gauges for the largest positive flows.
  2. Identify the Sector Gauges that are most positive and least extended to further enhance probabilities of gains.
  3. Lastly, confirm the positive MDA breakout signals on individual stocks in the best sector and best market conditions to maximize returns.

I wish you well in all your trading decisions,

JD Henning, PhD

data.title: PNC Financial: Not A Great Buy Right Now
data.description:
The PNC Financial Services Group (PNC) exceeded EPS analyst estimates for the first fiscal quarter last week, but missed slightly on revenue expectations. The regional bank completed its transaction with FirstBank in the first quarter, which implies that PNC Financial Services will now be able to expand its balance sheet and grow its deposits and loans, especially in growth areas like Colorado and Arizona.

Additionally, PNC Financial Services benefited from higher net interest income in Q1 '26 -- driven by favorable mix shifts and favorable asset repricing. While inflation recently soared to 3.3% in March, there are indications that the war between the U.S. and Iran may be winding down, which could create an incentive for the Federal Reserve to resume its rate-cutting policy. From a valuation perspective, I believe that PNC Financial Services is about fully valued, and I would recommend investors wait for a drop before establishing a new position in the regional bank.

Previous rating

In my last review on the regional banking platform, I advised investors to hold shares of PNC specifically because of PNC Financial Services' proposed acquisition of FirstBank. The acquisition target at the time was an opportunity for PNC Financial Services to expand its business in the important Denver area, but the risk of potential interest rate cuts counterbalanced my view on the bank: Limited Upside In 2026.

While the Federal Reserve did not lower federal fund rates in March, recent developments in the Middle East suggest that we could see rate cuts in 2026. Despite a healthy pace of loan expansion and higher NII, I see shares of PNC, both historically, as well as, in comparison to other regional banks, as fairly valued.

FirstBank Acquisition makes an impact on PNC Financial in Q1 '26

PNC Financial Services closed the acquisition of FirstBank on January 5, 2026, which ended up adding approximately $15.5B in loans and $22.0B in deposits to the regional bank's balance sheet.
